title: Amp Futures Vs Tradovate Vs Optimus: Cheapest Futures Broker In 2026
meta_title: Cheapest Futures Broker 2026: AMP vs Tradovate vs Optimus
meta_description: Compare AMP Futures ($0.44/contract), Tradovate (commission-free plans), and Optimus Futures ($0.25/side) to find the cheapest futures broker in 2026.
excerpt: Side-by-side cost breakdown of AMP Futures, Tradovate, and Optimus Futures — the three cheapest futures brokers in 2026, ranked by volume.
content:
The Real Cost of Futures Trading in 2026

Futures trading costs aren't just commissions — they're the sum of commissions, platform fees, data fees, exchange fees, and minimum account requirements. Most broker comparison articles gloss over this. This one doesn't.

Three brokers consistently come up when traders search for the cheapest futures broker in 2026: AMP Futures, Tradovate, and Optimus Futures. All three are genuinely low-cost options — but they price their services differently, and the cheapest choice for a trader doing 20 contracts a day looks very different from one doing 200.

This article gives you actual numbers, not marketing language. We'll break down what each broker charges, when each pricing model wins, and which broker makes sense for your specific situation.

One important note before the numbers: every futures broker charges exchange fees on top of their own commissions. These are set by CME Group and other exchanges — not the broker — and they're identical no matter where you trade. What matters for comparison purposes is strictly the broker's own commission layer.

Quick Comparison: AMP Futures vs Tradovate vs Optimus

Here's the data you need at a glance before we go deeper:

BrokerRatingMin. DepositMicro Futures RateMonthly Platform FeePlatforms
AMP Futures4.2/5$100~$0.44/contract (MES)$060+
Tradovate4.0/5Varies by plan$0 commission (membership)$0–$99+/mo1 (cloud)
Optimus Futures4.3/5Varies$0.25/side (micro contracts)$08+

Optimus earns the highest rating (4.3/5) and the lowest stated per-contract rate. AMP wins on accessibility with a $100 minimum deposit and the broadest platform support in the industry. Tradovate's commission-free membership model is compelling but only pays off at sufficient trading volume. Each section below goes into the specifics.

AMP Futures: 60+ Platforms, $100 to Start

AMP Futures built its reputation on one thing: supporting more trading platforms than anyone else. With 60+ compatible platforms — including NinjaTrader, Sierra Chart, Quantower, Bookmap, Jigsaw, MultiCharts, and many more — AMP is the default choice for traders who already have a platform they trust and don't want to abandon it.

For micro futures traders, AMP charges approximately $0.44 per contract on MES (Micro E-mini S&P 500) futures. There are no monthly account fees charged by AMP, and the minimum deposit is just $100 — the lowest in the industry. This combination makes AMP one of the most accessible on-ramps into live futures trading.

The platform-agnostic model is a genuine advantage, but it does come with a setup cost. AMP doesn't offer a branded mobile app or proprietary desktop environment — you connect a third-party platform, configure your data feed, and manage the relationship separately. For experienced traders, this is a non-issue. For beginners expecting a turnkey experience, it adds friction.

AMP's US entity is restricted to futures only — no stocks, spot forex, or spot crypto. If you want to understand how AMP compares to other FCMs in terms of clearing relationships and back-office structure, the AMP Futures vs Dorman Trading comparison is worth reading.

  • Best for: Active day traders already using NinjaTrader, Sierra Chart, or any third-party platform
  • Best for: Small account traders — $100 gets you live with real money
  • Not ideal for: Traders who want a single integrated platform without external configuration

Tradovate: Commission-Free Futures With a Membership Model

Tradovate takes a fundamentally different approach to pricing. Instead of charging per contract, it offers membership-based commission-free trading: pay a flat monthly fee, and your broker-side commission drops to zero. You still pay exchange fees — those are unavoidable — but the per-contract brokerage charge disappears.

The platform itself is cloud-based, which means it runs in a browser or desktop app without requiring a separate data feed or software installation. Your charts, indicators, settings, and positions are tied to your account, not your machine. For traders who work from multiple computers or travel regularly, this is a real practical benefit. Full API access is also included, making Tradovate attractive for algorithmic traders who need to connect automated strategies programmatically.

The critical question with Tradovate is whether your trading volume justifies the membership cost. On a pay-per-trade basis (no membership), Tradovate's per-contract rate is competitive but not the lowest of the three brokers here. The commission-free membership only wins financially once your monthly volume generates enough saved commissions to exceed the membership fee. A trader executing 10 contracts per week may not break even. A trader doing 30+ contracts per day almost certainly will.

Tradovate's key limitation is its closed ecosystem. You're restricted to Tradovate's own platform — no Sierra Chart, no Bookmap, no NinjaTrader. And unlike some competing platforms, there's no backtesting engine for testing rule-based strategies on historical data before deploying them live.

  • Best for: High-frequency futures traders whose volume clears the membership break-even point
  • Best for: Algo and automated traders who need full programmatic API access
  • Not ideal for: Low-volume traders, or anyone who needs external charting or backtesting tools

Optimus Futures: Lowest Stated Rate and the Highest Rating

Optimus Futures earns a 4.3/5 rating — the highest of the three — and it offers the lowest stated per-contract rate for micro futures at $0.25 per side. On a round-turn basis (entry + exit), that's $0.50 per micro contract before exchange fees. On MES specifically, this is among the lowest all-in brokerage rates available to retail traders.

What distinguishes Optimus beyond price is its multi-platform model. The broker provides free simultaneous access to 8+ professional platforms, including TradingView, Sierra Chart, and Bookmap — all at no extra cost. If you want to watch Bookmap for order flow analysis, run your execution through Sierra Chart, and monitor a TradingView watchlist simultaneously, Optimus supports that without charging platform fees for any of them. This is a meaningful operational advantage for traders who use layered tools.

The broker is limited to futures and options on futures — no spot forex, equities, or equity options. If you ever trade outside of futures, you'll need a separate account elsewhere. The other notable drawback is wire withdrawal fees, which are reportedly higher than the industry average. If you make frequent withdrawals, factor this into your total cost calculation.

For traders evaluating Optimus in the context of the broader futures broker landscape, the AMP Futures vs Jigsaw Trading comparison provides useful context on how order flow platforms and clearing firms interact in practice.

  • Best for: Micro futures traders (MES, MNQ, MCL, M2K) focused on minimizing per-contract cost
  • Best for: Multi-platform traders who want TradingView, Sierra Chart, and Bookmap without juggling multiple broker relationships
  • Not ideal for: Traders who make frequent wire withdrawals or need a single integrated all-in-one solution

Cost Breakdown by Volume in 2026: Which Broker Wins?

Using MES (Micro E-mini S&P 500) as the benchmark and excluding exchange fees (identical across brokers), here's how costs compare at different trading volumes:

Low Volume: Under 100 Round-Turns/Month

At low volumes, Tradovate's membership cost is difficult to justify unless you're on a pay-per-trade plan. Between AMP ($0.44/contract) and Optimus ($0.50/round-turn), AMP is marginally cheaper per trade — $44 vs. $50 at 100 round-turns. However, Optimus's platform access advantage may offset this for traders who rely on TradingView or Sierra Chart.

Medium Volume: 100–500 Round-Turns/Month

At 500 round-turns per month, AMP costs $220 in commissions vs. $250 for Optimus. AMP pulls ahead on pure cost as volume increases. At this level, the choice between the two largely comes down to which platforms you need — AMP's 60+ options vs. Optimus's 8+ with zero platform fees on all of them.

High Volume: 500+ Round-Turns/Month

At this level, Tradovate's membership model deserves a serious calculation. If your monthly commission spend at Optimus or AMP's per-contract rates would exceed Tradovate's membership fee, switching to the commission-free plan generates savings. Tradovate wins for high-frequency traders once volume clears the break-even threshold — particularly for algo traders who value the API integration.

Which Futures Broker Is Actually the Cheapest? Our Take

There's no single winner because the right answer depends on how you trade. Here's the decision framework:

Choose Optimus Futures if you trade micro futures and want the lowest stated per-contract rate. At $0.25/side with zero platform fees and free access to TradingView, Sierra Chart, and Bookmap simultaneously, Optimus delivers the best cost structure for most retail micro futures traders. The 4.3/5 rating reflects that it executes well on its promises.

Choose AMP Futures if platform flexibility is your priority or if you're starting with a small account. The $100 minimum deposit is the lowest barrier to entry in the industry, and 60+ supported platforms means you're almost certainly able to use the tool you already know. AMP's ~$0.44/contract rate is competitive, and the absence of monthly fees makes the true cost easy to calculate.

Choose Tradovate if you're running high volume or automated strategies. Tradovate's commission-free membership model, cloud-based platform, and full API access are purpose-built for active algorithmic traders. Do the break-even math first — if your expected monthly commission spend at per-contract rates exceeds the membership fee, Tradovate wins on cost and adds operational benefits on top.

All three are legitimate, well-run brokers. The decision in 2026 comes down to volume, platform preferences, and how much the minimum deposit barrier matters to your situation. None of them will nickel-and-dime you with hidden fees the way legacy full-service brokers do — that part, at least, you can count on across the board.
